relevant_files.role: Core regex-based pattern engine; primary target for tree-sitter integration
SecurityScanner orchestrator; processes diffs and file content line-by-line
Pattern definitions (14 regex patterns); candidates for AST-based rewrites
Data structures for Finding, PatternDefinition, ValidationResult
Dependency manifest; shows regex, serde, no tree-sitter yet
Integration tests; includes multi-line detection limitation test
Development conventions: conventional commits, DCO, testing patterns

conventions.commits: Conventional commits with GPG signing and DCO sign-off required; format: type(scope): description
conventions.testing: Unit tests in module files (mod tests), integration tests in tests/ directory; AAA pattern; one happy path + one edge case per behavior
conventions.linting: cargo clippy -- -D warnings; cargo fmt --check; actionlint for workflows
conventions.error_handling: anyhow for applications (Result<T>), thiserror for libraries; Context trait for error context; LazyLock for lazy initialization

patterns: Embedded JSON configuration (patterns.json) loaded once via LazyLock
Regex-based line-by-line scanning with file extension filtering
Batched LLM validation for false positive reduction
Diff parsing with unified diff format support and line number tracking
SARIF output generation for standardized reporting

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Closed PR documenting architectural limitation: regex-based pattern matching cannot detect vulnerabilities spanning multiple lines (source and sink on different lines); directly motivates tree-sitter evaluation

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Merged PR that adds test case documenting multi-line detection limitation; establishes baseline for tree-sitter improvement

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
related_issues.relevance: Target issue; proposes hybrid approach (regex for simple patterns, tree-sitter for data-flow); estimates 500-800 LOC, 14 pattern rewrites; suggests spike on tree-sitter-rust for SQL injection

constraints: Line-by-line regex scanning architecture cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities
Current pattern engine processes one line at a time; no cross-line context
Diff-based scanning requires mapping AST nodes back to diff line numbers
Binary size concerns with tree-sitter grammar dependencies
Grammar maintenance burden: each language requires separate grammar updates
test_coverage: 14 regex patterns with unit tests covering hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, path traversal, command injection, XSS, weak crypto, unsafe deserialization, XXE, insecure TLS, debug mode. Integration tests verify detection in fixture files. Known gap: multi-line vulnerability detection (documented in PR #736). Validator tests cover batched LLM validation and fallback to pattern confidence.

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser::new(), Tree::root_node(), Query API for pattern matching, language-specific parsers (tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, etc.)
library_findings.notes: Mature Rust bindings; stable API; supports incremental parsing; 1100+ language-specific crates available; requires separate grammar crates per language
Current implementation uses regex crate for all pattern matching; line-by-line iteration in PatternEngine::scan()
Used to deserialize patterns.json; existing pattern in codebase

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex+Tree-Sitter with Pluggable Backends
approaches.description: Refactor PatternEngine to support multiple backends (regex, tree-sitter) per pattern. Add a backend field to PatternDefinition JSON. Create trait PatternMatcher with implementations for RegexMatcher and TreeSitterMatcher. Rewrite 3-5 critical patterns (SQL injection, command injection, unsafe deserialization) to use tree-sitter AST queries. Keep remaining patterns on regex for simplicity. Patterns.json specifies which backend to use per pattern.
pros: Gradual migration: can rewrite patterns incrementally
Leverages existing regex infrastructure for simple patterns
Reduces complexity by targeting only high-impact patterns (SQL injection, command injection)
Allows A/B testing: regex vs tree-sitter results on same codebase
Maintains backward compatibility with existing pattern format
cons: Adds complexity: two execution paths in PatternEngine
Requires grammar maintenance for each language (tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, etc.)
AST node-to-line mapping adds overhead; must track source ranges
Tree-sitter grammar updates lag language updates

approaches.name: Dedicated AST Scanner Module with Separate Pipeline
approaches.description: Create new security/ast_scanner.rs module alongside patterns.rs. Implement AstScanner struct using tree-sitter for multi-line patterns (SQL injection, command injection, data-flow). Keep existing PatternEngine for single-line patterns. SecurityScanner calls both engines sequentially, deduplicates findings. Patterns.json remains regex-only; new ast_patterns.json defines tree-sitter queries. Validator layer deduplicates and ranks findings.
pros: Clean separation of concerns: regex and AST scanners are independent
Lower risk: existing regex pipeline unchanged
Can develop and test AST scanner in isolation
Easier to deprecate regex patterns later
Allows independent versioning of pattern sets
cons: Duplicate pattern definitions (regex and AST queries)
Validator must handle overlapping findings from two sources
Larger binary (tree-sitter + grammars for multiple languages)
Estimated 700-900 LOC; requires new pattern format

approaches.name: Tree-Sitter-First Refactor with Regex Fallback
approaches.description: Replace PatternEngine with TreeSitterEngine that uses tree-sitter for all patterns. Rewrite all 14 patterns as tree-sitter queries (one per language grammar). For languages without tree-sitter support, fall back to regex. Update patterns.json to include tree-sitter query field alongside regex pattern. PatternEngine becomes thin wrapper that selects backend based on file extension and language availability.
pros: Unified pattern format (tree-sitter queries as primary)
Enables true multi-line and data-flow analysis for all patterns
Better long-term maintainability: AST-based rules are more precise
cons: High complexity: requires rewriting all 14 patterns as tree-sitter queries
Significant binary size increase (tree-sitter + grammars for 6+ languages)
Grammar maintenance burden; must track language grammar updates
Potential performance regression if not optimized (AST parsing slower than regex for simple patterns)
Breaking change to pattern format; migration effort for users
Estimated 800-1200 LOC; 14 pattern rewrites; 2-3 month effort

recommendation: Approach 1 (Hybrid Regex+Tree-Sitter with Pluggable Backends) is recommended. It balances impact with maintainability: targets the highest-value patterns (SQL injection, command injection) where multi-line analysis provides clear security benefit, while preserving the existing regex infrastructure for simpler patterns. This approach aligns with the issue's suggested spike on tree-sitter-rust for SQL injection. It can be delivered incrementally (spike -> prototype -> production), allows measuring impact before full migration, and maintains backward compatibility. Start with SQL injection and command injection patterns, measure false positive reduction via the validator, then expand to other patterns based on ROI. Approach 3 is elegant but too risky for the current codebase maturity; Approach 2 adds unnecessary complexity with duplicate pattern definitions.
